volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Captives (Section 4.1.775 - Section 4.2.857)

sections.label: Section 4.1.775
sections.original: síne sacris hereditatem sum aptus effertissimam .
sections.translation: I have lighted upon a most rich inheritance without incumbrances . [*] Now will I wend my way to this old gentleman Hegio , to whom I am carrying blessings as great as he himself prays for from the Gods , and even greater . Now , this is my determination , in the same fashion that the slaves of Comedy [*] are wont , so will I throw my cloak around my neck , that from me , the first of all , he may learn this matter .

sections.label: Section 4.1.780
sections.original: speróque me ob hunc nuntium aetérnum adepturum cibum .
sections.translation: And I trust that I , by reason of this news , shall find provision up to the end .

sections.label: Section 4.2.781
sections.original: Quanto ín pectore hánc rem meó magis volúto ,
sections.translation: The more that I revolve this matter in my breast , the more is my uneasiness of mind increased . That I should have been duped in this fashion to-day ! and that I wasn ’ t able to see through it !

sections.label: Section 4.2.785
sections.original: quod cúm scibitúr , tum per úrbem inridébor .
sections.translation: When this shall be known , then I shall be laughed at all over the city . The very moment that I shall have reached the Forum , all will be saying , This is that clever old gentleman , who had the trick played him . But is this Ergasilus , that I see coming at a distance ? Surely he has got his cloak gathered up ; what , I wonder , is he going to do ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.790
sections.original: Move áps te moram átque , Ergásile , age hanc rem .
sections.translation: Throw aside from you all tardiness , Ergasilus , and speed on this business . I threaten , and I strictly charge no person to stand in my way , unless any one shall be of opinion that he has lived long enough . For whoever does come in my way , shall stop me upon his face .

sections.label: Section 4.2.793b
sections.original: Hic homo pugilatum incipit .
sections.translation: This fellow ’ s beginning to box .

sections.label: Section 4.2.794
sections.original: Facere certumst . proinde ita omnes itinera insistant sua ,
sections.translation: I ’ m determined to do it ; so that every one may pursue his own path ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.795
sections.original: ne quis in hanc plateam negoti conferat quicquam sui .
sections.translation: let no one be bringing any of his business in this street ; for my fist is a balista , my arm is my catapulta , my shoulder a battering-ram ; then against whomsoever I dart my knee , I shall bring him to the ground . I ’ ll make all persons to be picking up their teeth [*] , whomsoever I shall meet with .

sections.label: Section 4.2.799
sections.original: Quae illaec eminatiost nam ? nequeo mirari satis .
sections.translation: What threatening is this ? For I cannot wonder enough .

sections.label: Section 4.2.800
sections.original: Faciam ut huius diei locique meique semper meminerit .
sections.translation: I ’ ll make him always to remember this day and place , and myself as well . Whoever stops me upon my road , I ’ ll make him put a stop to his own existence .

sections.label: Section 4.2.802
sections.original: Quid hic homo tantum incipissit facere cum tantis minis ?
sections.translation: What great thing is this fellow preparing to do , with such mighty threats ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.803
sections.original: Prius edico , ne quis propter culpam capiatur suam :
sections.translation: I first give notice , that no one , by reason of his own fault , may be caught — keep yourselves in-doors at home , and guard yourselves from my attack .

sections.label: Section 4.2.805
sections.original: Mira edepol sunt , ní hic in ventrem sumpsit confidentiam .
sections.translation: By my faith , ‘ tis strange if he hasn ’ t got this boldness by means of his stomach . Woe to that wretched man , through whose cheer this fellow has become quite swaggering .

sections.label: Section 4.2.807
sections.original: Tum pistores scrofipasci , quí alunt furfuribus sues ,
sections.translation: Then the bakers , that feed swine , that fatten their pigs upon refuse bran , through the stench of which no one can pass by a baker ’ s shop ; if I see the pig of any one of them in the public way ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.810
sections.original: ex ipsis dominis meis pugnis exculcabo furfures .
sections.translation: I ’ ll beat the bran out of the masters ’ themselves with my fists .

sections.label: Section 4.2.811
sections.original: Basilicas edictiones atque imperiosas habet :
sections.translation: Royal and imperial edicts does he give out . The fellow is full ; he certainly has his boldness from his stomach .

sections.label: Section 4.2.813
sections.original: Tum piscatores , qui praebent populo pisces foetidos ,
sections.translation: Then the fishmongers , who supply stinking fish to the public — who are carried about on a gelding , with his galloping galling pace [*] —

sections.label: Section 4.2.815
sections.original: quorum odos subbasilicanos omnes abigit in forum ,
sections.translation: the stench of whom drives all the loungers in the Basilica [*] into the Forum , I ’ ll bang their heads with their bulrush fish-baskets , that they may understand what annoyance they cause to the noses of other people . And then the butchers , as well , who render the sheep destitute of their young — who agree with you about killing lamb [*] , and then offer you lamb at double the price —

sections.label: Section 4.2.820
sections.original: qui petroni nomen indunt verveci sectario ,
sections.translation: who give the name of wether mutton to a ram — if I should only see that ram in the public way , I ’ ll make both ram and owner most miserable beings .

sections.label: Section 4.2.823
sections.original: Eugepae , edictiones aedilicias hic quidem habet ,
sections.translation: Well done ! He really does give out edicts fit for an Aedile , and ‘ tis indeed a surprising thing if the Aetolians haven ’ t made him inspector of markets [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.825
sections.original: Non ego nunc parasitus sum , sed regum rex regalior ,
sections.translation: No Parasite now am I , but a right royal king of kings ; so large a stock of provision for my stomach is there at hand in the harbour . But why delay to overwhelm this old gentleman Hegio with gladness ? With him , not a person among mankind exists equally fortunate .

sections.label: Section 4.2.829
sections.original: Quae illaec est laetitia , quam illic laetus largitur mihi ?
sections.translation: What joy is this , that he , thus joyous , is going to impart to me ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.830
sections.original: Heus ubi estis ? ecquis hic est ? ecquis hoc aperit ostium ?
sections.translation: Hallo , hallo !— where are you ? Is any one coming to open this door ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.831
sections.original: Hic homo ad cenam recipit se ad me .
sections.translation: This fellow ’ s betaking himself to my house to dine .

sections.label: Section 4.2.831b
sections.original: Áperite hasce ambas fores
sections.translation: Open you both these doors [*] , before I shall with knocking cause the destruction , piecemeal , of the doors .

sections.label: Section 4.2.833
sections.original: Perlúbet hunc hominem colloqui . Ergásile .
sections.translation: I ’ d like much to address the fellow . ( Aloud .) Ergasilus !

sections.label: Section 4.2.833b
sections.original: Ergasilum qui vocat ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s calling Ergasilus ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.834
sections.original: Respíce .
sections.translation: Turn round , and look at me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.834b
sections.original: Fortuna quod tibi nec fácit nec faciet , me iubes .
sections.translation: A thing that Fortune does not do for you , nor ever will do , you bid me to do .

sections.label: Section 4.2.835
sections.original: séd quis est ?
sections.translation: But who is it .

sections.label: Section 4.2.835b
sections.original: Réspice ad me , Hégio sum .
sections.translation: Look round at me . ’ Tis Hegio .

sections.label: Section 4.2.835c
sections.original: Óh mihi ,
sections.translation: O me ! Best of the very best of men , as many as exist , you have arrived opportunely .

sections.label: Section 4.2.837
sections.original: Nescío quem ad portum náctus es ubi cénes , eo fastídis .
sections.translation: You ’ ve met with some one at the harbour to dine with ; through that you are elevated .

sections.label: Section 4.2.838
sections.original: Cédo manum .
sections.translation: Give me your hand .

sections.label: Section 4.2.838b
sections.original: Manum ?
sections.translation: My hand ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.838c
sections.original: Manum , inquam , cedo tuam actutum .
sections.translation: Give me your hand , I say , this instant .

sections.label: Section 4.2.838d
sections.original: Tene .
sections.translation: Take it .

sections.label: Section 4.2.839
sections.original: Gaude .
sections.translation: Rejoice .

sections.label: Section 4.2.839b
sections.original: Quid ego gaudeam ?
sections.translation: Why should I rejoice ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.839c
sections.original: Quia ego impero , age gaude modo .
sections.translation: Because I bid you ; come now , rejoice .

sections.label: Section 4.2.840
sections.original: Pol maerores mi antevortunt gaudiis .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , my sorrows exceed my rejoicings .

sections.label: Section 4.2.840b
sections.original: Noli irascier
sections.translation: ’ Tis not so , as you shall find ; I ’ ll at once drive away every spot of sorrow [*] from your body . Rejoice without restraint .

sections.label: Section 4.2.842b
sections.original: Gaudeo , etsi nil scio quod gaudeam .
sections.translation: I do rejoice , although I don ’ t at all know why I should rejoice .

sections.label: Section 4.2.843
sections.original: Bene facis . iubé —
sections.translation: You do rightly ; now order —

sections.label: Section 4.2.843b
sections.original: Quid iubeam ?
sections.translation: Order what ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.843c
sections.original: Ignem ingentem fieri .
sections.translation: A large fire to be made .

sections.label: Section 4.2.844
sections.original: Ignem ingentem ?
sections.translation: A large fire ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.844b
sections.original: Ita dico , magnus ut sit .
sections.translation: So I say , that a huge one it must be .

sections.label: Section 4.2.844c
sections.original: Quid ? me , volturi ,
sections.translation: What , you vulture , do you suppose that for your sake I ’ m going to set my house on fire ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.845b
sections.original: Noli irascier .
sections.translation: Don ’ t be angry . Will you order , or will you not order , the pots to be put on , and the saucepans to be washed out , the bacon and the dainties to be made warm in the heated cooking-stoves , another one , too , to go purchase the fish ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.848b
sections.original: Hic vigilans somniat .
sections.translation: This fellow ’ s dreaming while awake .

sections.label: Section 4.2.849
sections.original: Alium porcinam atque agninam et pullos gallinaceos ?
sections.translation: Another to buy pork , and lamb , and pullets .

sections.label: Section 4.2.850
sections.original: Scis bene esse , si sit unde .
sections.translation: You understand how to feed well , if you had the means .

sections.label: Section 4.2.850b
sections.original: † Pernam atque ophthalmiam ,
sections.translation: Gammons of bacon , too , and lampreys , spring pickled tunny-fish , mackerel , and sting-ray ; large fish , too , and soft cheese .

sections.label: Section 4.2.852
sections.original: Nominandi istorum tibi erit magis quam edundi copia
sections.translation: You will have more opportunity , Ergasilus , here at my house , of talking about these things than of eating them .

sections.label: Section 4.2.853b
sections.original: Mean me causa hoc censes dicere ?
sections.translation: Do you suppose that I ’ m saying this on my own account ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.854
sections.original: Nec nihil hodie nec multo plus tu hic edes , ne frustra sis .
sections.translation: You will neither be eating nothing here to-day , nor yet much more than usual , so don ’ t you be mistaken .

sections.label: Section 4.2.855
sections.original: proin tu tui cottidiani victi ventrem ad me afferas .
sections.translation: Do you then bring an appetite to my house for your every-day fare .

sections.label: Section 4.2.856
sections.original: Quin ita faciam , ut tu te cupias facere sumptum , etsi ego vetem .
sections.translation: Why , I ’ ll so manage it , that you yourself shall wish to be profuse , though I myself should desire you not .

sections.label: Section 4.2.857
sections.original: Egone ?
sections.translation: What , I ?
